title: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
author: Sam Riley
orientation: Sam Riley argues that Confederate theologian R.L. Dabney was a sinful defender of race-based chattel slavery yet, paradoxically, articulated a textbook case for biblical immediatism—conceding that if slavery were truly sin, every Christian was bound to be a thorough abolitionist. Riley uses Dabney's own logic to indict modern pro-life incrementalism and figures like Doug Wilson and John MacArthur who lean on Dabney's authority.
audiences: Reformed Christian sympathetic to Doug Wilson or John MacArthur
Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
Skeptic suspicious of abolitionists for citing Confederate apologists
Pastor or theologian wrestling with how to handle morally compromised historical figures
Abolitionist activist looking for historical and biblical ammunition against gradualism

questions.question: How can abolitionists cite a Confederate apologist like Dabney without endorsing his racism?
questions.audience: Skeptic suspicious of abolitionists for citing Confederate apologists
questions.answer: Riley is unsparing about Dabney's sin: he calls Dabney's views toward black people 'rebellion against God and sin against Dabney's black contemporaries' and devotes the first half of the article to documenting his white supremacy in detail. The point of citing Dabney is precisely that even a man this morally compromised on slavery saw the logical force of immediatism—if anyone should listen to Dabney's argument for abolitionism, it is the modern incrementalist who already shares his sympathy.
questions.quote: There is much to appreciate about Dabney, who was a skilled writer and insightful theologian on many subjects. But his views toward black people were rebellion against God and sin against Dabney's black contemporaries. This is no small matter.

questions.question: What exactly did Dabney concede that makes him 'right on abolitionism'?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: Dabney conceded that the only thing keeping him from being a thorough abolitionist was his belief that the Bible permitted slavery. Strip that premise away and his own reasoning forced him into immediatism: a moral evil cannot be lawfully continued for the sake of convenience or feasibility, and consequences are God's business while duty is ours.
questions.quote: If I did not believe that the bible taught [that slavery was morally innocent], **I must, in consistency,** **be a thorough abolitionist**. I cannot see how men can say in one breath, that slavery is a *malum per se* and in the next, **that a conscientious man may lawfully continue it for the present, because of the difficulties of emancipation**.

questions.question: Why is immediatism a biblical demand and not just a political strategy?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian wrestling with how to handle morally compromised historical figures
questions.answer: Riley argues that Scripture knows no scheme of partial repentance—God demands total and immediate obedience. Abolition bills are simply the legal expression of the prophetic call to repent, while incremental bills assume a posture of submission to the Supreme Court rather than to Christ enthroned. Even Dabney conceded the principle: sin must be ceased at once.
questions.quote: My conscience and my bible teach me that, if an act is *wrong*, in its own essential nature, sin, **I am to cease it at once**. I have no right to look at the supposed evil consequences or difficulties of the reformation.

questions.question: Doesn't the difficulty of abolishing abortion all at once justify a more cautious legislative approach?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolitionism
questions.answer: Riley says no—and uses Dabney's adultery analogy to make the point. Telling a man he should not leave an adulterous relationship because doing so would be inconvenient or dangerous is absurd; the same logic applies to abortion. Faithfulness commits the consequences to God: 'Fiat justitia, ruat cælum'—let justice be done though the heavens fall.
questions.quote: What would be the thought of the man, who should plead that he ought not to cease living in an adulterous connexion, because a change would be dangerous and inconvenient?—Would not you answer, "unless you cease that connexion at every risk, you are an immoral man?"

questions.question: How does Riley critique Doug Wilson specifically given Wilson's reliance on Dabney?
questions.audience: Reformed Christian sympathetic to Doug Wilson or John MacArthur
questions.answer: Riley notes that Wilson cites *A Defence of Virginia* in *Black and Tan* and even credits Dabney with persuading him to revise his view of the Civil War. Yet Wilson refuses to apply Dabney's own immediatist logic to abortion, instead warning that Idaho would face federal troops if it abolished. Riley charges that Wilson's persistent emphasis on consequences scares Christians away from abolition—a faithless posture Dabney himself would have rejected.
questions.quote: Men like Doug Wilson would do well to listen to Dabney in this narrow regard, but instead Wilson says things like, "if anybody thinks they would let Idaho [abolish abortion]… without sending in the troops, is just out of their mind."

questions.question: How is the inconsistency of pro-life legislation parallel to the inconsistency of gradualist anti-slavery?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for historical and biblical ammunition against gradualism
questions.answer: Dabney pressed gradualists by arguing that if they really believed slavery was wrong, they were obligated to immediate abolition; treating an evil as evil while legislating as though it were not is incoherent. Riley applies the same razor to pro-life Christians: professing that abortion is murder while passing bills that regulate, license, or partially permit it preaches with one's actions that abortion is healthcare.
questions.quote: The Christian, to be consistent, must not profess with his mouth that abortion is murder and preach with his actions and his legislative efforts that abortion is healthcare. That is what pro-life legislation does.

questions.question: What does it look like to act 'by faith' on abolition rather than by political calculation?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist looking for historical and biblical ammunition against gradualism
questions.answer: Riley grounds the answer in Hebrews 11—the saints who conquered kingdoms, escaped the sword, were sawn in two, and went about destitute did so by faith without weighing whether the consequences were tolerable. Modern abolitionists are called to the same posture: demand officials pass and enforce bills that defy *Roe v. Wade* regardless of judicial reprisal.
questions.quote: By faith, abolitionists demand our governing officials pass and enforce bills that defy *Roe v. Wade*, consequences be damned and God be praised. Wilson's endorsement of incremental measures must be rejected on the grounds that they are not only unjust, but they are faithless.

questions.question: Should John MacArthur's recommendation of Dabney trouble his Reformed admirers?
questions.audience: Reformed Christian sympathetic to Doug Wilson or John MacArthur
questions.answer: Riley flags that MacArthur speaks highly of Dabney without ever mentioning Dabney's 'unabashed white supremacy-supporting publications.' That silence matters because Dabney's racism was not a side issue—it infected even his systematic theology, where he denied the African in America a natural right to self-control over labor and locomotion. Recommending Dabney without confronting that record sanitizes a heresy that denies the gospel's power to sanctify.
questions.quote: Notably, reformed pastors Doug Wilson and John MacArthur have both spoken highly of Dabney, with the latter not mentioning Dabney's unabashed white supremacy-supporting publications and the former sometimes relying on them for his own writings.

questions.question: What is the article's bottom-line claim about how Dabney would treat abortion today?
questions.audience: Pastor or theologian wrestling with how to handle morally compromised historical figures
questions.answer: Riley contends that Dabney's own pen commits him to abortion abolitionism. If the Bible teaches abortion is wrong—and no orthodox Christian disputes this—then by Dabney's stated reasoning the Christian is bound to hold with the decided abolitionists, refusing to excuse continued injustice on grounds of expediency or inconvenience.
questions.quote: If the bible teaches that abortion is wrong, "we should be obliged to hold with the decided Abolitionists. We could never be of the number of those, who attempt to transmute the essential traits of moral right and wrong, at the demand of expediency, and to excuse the continuance of a radical injustice, by the inconvenience of repairing it. Duty belongs to man; consequences to God." – R.L. Dabney

related.title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
related.reason: A direct companion piece from the same month pressing Doug Wilson on the same incrementalism-vs-immediatism question Riley raises.

related.title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
related.reason: Extends the critique of the Wilson-Sumpter 'smashmouth incrementalism' framework that Riley's article challenges via Dabney.

related.title: Dang it, Doug.
related.reason: Another James Silberman piece taking on Doug Wilson's posture toward abolition and Roe v. Wade.

related.title: King Josiah's Smashmouth Abolitionism
related.reason: Develops the biblical case for immediate, total reform that Riley invokes against partial repentance.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Engages historical abolitionists like John Quincy Adams and William Lloyd Garrison whose 'consequences belong to God, duty belongs to us' principle Riley quotes through Dabney.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual
related.reason: Abolitionists Rising's primer on immediatism—the doctrinal position Riley argues Dabney accidentally defended.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Targets the same submission-to-the-Supreme-Court posture Riley identifies as the real driver of incremental pro-life bills.
